summary: Device policy for RDP clipboard redirection. The local Microsoft TerminalServer.admx file explicitly documents the same policy key, value name, and enabled/disabled values that the app uses.

setting.name: RDP Clipboard Redirection
setting.casual_explanation: Remote Desktop can let text and files move between your local clipboard and the remote machine. This policy controls whether that copy and paste bridge is allowed.
setting.why_people_change_it: Admins disable clipboard redirection for hardened remote sessions, kiosk devices, and compliance-sensitive environments. Power users often keep it enabled because it is convenient.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Blocking clipboard redirection can reduce accidental data movement between local and remote systems, but it also removes one of the most common RDP convenience features.
setting.professional_notes: This record describes the policy-backed fDisableClip value under Terminal Services. The local TerminalServer.admx file defines it under SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\Terminal Services with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0. It is a device policy, not a per-session preference.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: Windows does not force clipboard redirection behavior through policy.

allowed_values.label: Clipboard redirection allowed
allowed_values.meaning: Clipboard sharing between local and remote sessions is allowed.

allowed_values.label: Clipboard redirection blocked
allowed_values.meaning: Clipboard sharing between local and remote sessions is blocked.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes fDisableClip = 1, which matches the documented disable behavior.

windows_defaults.label: Windows policy default

states.rationale: Default policy state is not configured.

windows_defaults.notes: Default policy state does not guarantee the same behavior across every RDP scenario, but it does mean the device is not forcing the restriction through policy.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default

states.rationale: Keeps the machine at the normal Windows policy baseline.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want standard Remote Desktop behavior and do not need to enforce data movement restrictions.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need to stop users from copying data between local and remote machines.
tradeoffs: Clipboard copy and paste can still work across RDP boundaries.

recommended_profiles.label: Hardened RDP
avoid_for: Users who rely on copy and paste between systems
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to reduce the chance of content moving between the local machine and an RDP session.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: Copy and paste between systems is part of the workflow.
tradeoffs: Text and file copy and paste between local and remote sessions stops working.

recommended_profiles.label: RDP collaboration
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want to keep remote work convenient and predictable.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need stricter data separation between local and remote systems.
tradeoffs: Clipboard data can move between local and remote sessions.
This is a convenience-friendly profile, not a hardened one.

evidence.title: Microsoft Policy CSP ADMX TerminalServer: Disable clipboard redirection
evidence.summary: Official documentation for the Terminal Services policy that maps to fDisableClip.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft TerminalServer.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file shows TS_CLIENT_CLIPBOARD under SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\Terminal Services with valueName fDisableClip, enabledValue 1, and disabledValue 0.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes fDisableClip = 1 for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Existing tweak source record
evidence.summary: Current repo provenance already links this tweak to nohuto references and internal audit output.

decision.why: The local Microsoft TerminalServer.admx file explicitly defines fDisableClip under SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\Terminal Services with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0, which matches the app's device-policy write.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: <policy name="TS_CLIENT_CLIPBOARD" ... key="SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\Terminal Services" valueName="fDisableClip"> ... <enabledValue><decimal value="1" /></enabledValue> <disabledValue><decimal value="0" /></disabledValue>
validation_proof.notes: The local official Microsoft ADMX file names the same policy key and fDisableClip value used by the app, and it confirms the 1/0 mapping.
